description:
You are an infrastructure engineer in the middle of automating a new provisioning workflow.  
    Management wants **user-level** timers (not system timers) so that every engineer can keep the
    service inside their own `$HOME` without touching `/etc`.

    Goal  
    ────  
    1. Author a **systemd user service** called **hello-date.service** and a matching
       **hello-date.timer** that runs the service every 10 minutes.  
    2. Deliver a tiny “proof-of-creation” log so QA can non-interactively grade your work.

    Functional requirements  
    ───────────────────────  
    • Location of units: **/home/user/.config/systemd/user/**  
      (Create the directory hierarchy if it does not already exist.)  

    • hello-date.service — exact file contents **must** be  

        [Unit]
        Description=Write current date to /home/user/date_logs/hello.log

        [Service]
        Type=oneshot
        ExecStart=/usr/bin/env bash -c 'date "+%F %T" >> /home/user/date_logs/hello.log'

      The file **must end with a single UNIX newline** and contain **no trailing spaces** on any line.  

    • hello-date.timer — exact file contents **must** be  

        [Unit]
        Description=Timer for hello-date.service (writes timestamp to log every 10 minutes)

        [Timer]
        OnCalendar=*:0/10
        Unit=hello-date.service
        Persistent=true

        [Install]
        WantedBy=timers.target

      This file must also end with a single UNIX newline and have no trailing spaces.  

    • Do not worry about enabling or starting the timer; unit authoring is enough.  
      (There is no systemd in the grading container, only file-level checks.)  

    Verification artifact  
    ─────────────────────  
    After creating the two unit files, write **/home/user/task_verification/created_files.log**  
    with **exactly two lines, in ASCII sort order, terminated by a single final newline**:

        /home/user/.config/systemd/user/hello-date.service
        /home/user/.config/systemd/user/hello-date.timer

    QA’s automated tests will:  
    1. Read the log file to discover which files you created.  
    2. Compare the on-disk contents to the specifications above byte-for-byte.  

    Non-functional constraints  
    ──────────────────────────  
    • You do NOT have root; work only under /home/user.  
    • Use standard GNU/Linux user-space tools available in a minimal Debian/Ubuntu environment.  
    • When you finish, ensure no additional output is printed to stdout/stderr other than what
      your commands naturally emit while creating the files.  (The grader will ignore stdout,
      but please keep it tidy.)

    Tip: a single call to `mkdir -p` and two uses of `cat > … <<'EOF'` (or similar redirection)  
    are enough to satisfy the whole task.
truth:
<!--
        Ground-truth data used by the autograder.
        These values MUST remain secret from the agent.
    -->

    <!-- Path variables -->
    <path.service>/home/user/.config/systemd/user/hello-date.service</path.service>
    <path.timer>/home/user/.config/systemd/user/hello-date.timer</path.timer>
    <path.log>/home/user/task_verification/created_files.log</path.log>

    <!-- Expected file contents (each ends with one '\n') -->
    <content.service>[Unit]
Description=Write current date to /home/user/date_logs/hello.log

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/usr/bin/env bash -c 'date "+%F %T" >> /home/user/date_logs/hello.log'
</content.service>

    <content.timer>[Unit]
Description=Timer for hello-date.service (writes timestamp to log every 10 minutes)

[Timer]
OnCalendar=*:0/10
Unit=hello-date.service
Persistent=true

[Install]
WantedBy=timers.target
</content.timer>

    <!-- Expected verification log (ends with one '\n') -->
    <content.verification>/home/user/.config/systemd/user/hello-date.service
/home/user/.config/systemd/user/hello-date.timer
</content.verification>
